coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots mediated silver phosphate/bismuth vanadate Z-scheme photocatalyst for enhanced antibiotic degradation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jin
Yan, Ming
Yuan, Xingzhong
Si, Mengying
Jiang, Longbo
Wu, Zhibin
Wang, Hou
Zeng, Guangming
coredata.dc:description: A novel nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) mediated Ag3PO4/BiVO4 Z-scheme photocatalyst was synthesized through a solvothermal-precipitation method. The as-prepared photocatalysts were comprehensive characterized by X-ray diffraction, X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron micrograph, UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy, vis photoluminescence and electron spin resonance analysis. The photocatalytic performances of as-prepared photocatalysts were used for degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible-light illumination. Results exhibited the increased photocatalytic efficiency of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10 (Ag3PO4 with the mass ratio of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 = 10%) to photodegrade TC is up to 88.9% in 30 min and 59.8% mineralization in 90 min. The degradation reaction coefficient (k) is about 6.00, 2.78 and 1.80 times higher than that of BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4 and Ag3PO4/BiVO4, respectively. The excellent photocatalytic performance of the Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 was attributed to the construction of Z-scheme system, increased light harvesting capacity and improved molecular oxygen activation ability. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 remained 79.9% after five cycling runs, indicating the high stability and reusability of the as-prepared photocatalyst. Additionally, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of the novel Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 was proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiVO4
N-CQDs
Ag3PO4
Tetracycline
Visible light photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4/BiVO4 composites
Morphology and textural property of photocatalysts

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51521006, No. 51739004 and No. 21776066).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the pure BiVO4 and modified BiVO4 hybrid materials.
XPS spectra of the pure BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4: (a) survey spectrum, (b) Bi 4f, (c) V 2p, (d) O 1s, (e) Ag 3d, (f) P 2p, (g) N 1s and (h) C 1s.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) Raman spectra of the BiVO4 and modified BiVO4 hybrid materials.
SEM images of (a, d) BiVO4, (b, e) Ag3PO4 and (c, f) BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4.
SEM-EDS elemental mapping images of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials.
(a) TEM image and (d) HRTEM image of BiVO4. (b) TEM image and (e) HRTEM image of Ag3PO4. (c) TEM image and (f) HRTEM image of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials.
(a) UV–vis spectra of the as-prepared samples. (b) Plots of (αhυ)2 versus (hυ) for BiVO4. (c) Plots of (αhυ)1/2 versus (hυ) for Ag3PO4. (d) PL spectra of the as-prepared samples.
(a) Photocurrent and (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the BiVO4, Ag3PO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials under visible light irradiation.
(a) Comparison of photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared samples on the degradation of TC under visible light irradiation. (b) Kinetics of TC degradation reaction over the as-prepared samples.

(a) Recyclability of the photocatalytic decomposition of TC by BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials; (b) XRD patterns of the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials before and after the cycling photocatalytic experiments.
Trapping experiment of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of TC over BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials under visible light irradiation.

ESR spectra of radical adducts trapped by DMPO (
O2 − and
OH) in BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 in the dark and under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm): (a) in methanol dispersion for DMPO-
O2 −; and (b) in aqueous dispersion for DMPO-
OH.
Schematic illustration of the proposed mechanism for photoexcited electron-hole separation in the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials under visible light irradiation.
TOC analysis of TC degradation by BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10 under visible irradiation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The standard error referred to that of the concentration of TOC.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots mediated silver phosphate/bismuth vanadate Z-scheme photocatalyst for enhanced antibiotic degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environment Biology and Pollution Control, Hunan University, Ministry of Education, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environment Biology and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy and Environment, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical & Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore 639798, Singapore

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) mediated Ag3PO4/BiVO4 Z-scheme photocatalyst was synthesized through a solvothermal-precipitation method. The as-prepared photocatalysts were comprehensive characterized by X-ray diffraction, X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron micrograph, UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy, vis photoluminescence and electron spin resonance analysis. The photocatalytic performances of as-prepared photocatalysts were used for degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible-light illumination. Results exhibited the increased photocatalytic efficiency of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10 (Ag3PO4 with the mass ratio of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 = 10%) to photodegrade TC is up to 88.9% in 30 min and 59.8% mineralization in 90 min. The degradation reaction coefficient (k) is about 6.00, 2.78 and 1.80 times higher than that of BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4 and Ag3PO4/BiVO4, respectively. The excellent photocatalytic performance of the Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 was attributed to the construction of Z-scheme system, increased light harvesting capacity and improved molecular oxygen activation ability. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 remained 79.9% after five cycling runs, indicating the high stability and reusability of the as-prepared photocatalyst. Additionally, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of the novel Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Over past decades, antibiotics, as the vital drugs for the treatment of pathogenic bacteria infection, were widely used across the world [1–4]. Nowadays, the antibiotics drawing a lot of attentions due to these antibiotics accumulated in the ecosystem may pose health threats to humans. Additionally, antibiotics have become a recalcitrant pollutant in the water system. Thus, removal of antibiotics from the environment, especially from the water, becoming an urgent issue [5].

In recent years, semiconductor-based photocatalysis to eliminate the pollution of antibiotics has heralded a new chapter [6–10]. It is imperative to develop the highly photoactive photocatalysts to make full use of the visible light (45% in the solar energy). Therefore, numerous visible light photocatalysts have been studied, for instance, C3N4, Bi2WO6, BiVO4, ZnO, WO3, In2S3, MoS2 and CdS [11–19]. Among them, bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) as a visible-light-driven photocatalyst for pollutant degradation has aroused increasing attention due to its narrow band gap of 2.4 eV, nontoxicity and relatively high stability [20]. Nevertheless, the photoactivity of pure BiVO4 is still weak because of the rapid photogenerated electron-hole recombination and poor visible light harvesting efficiency [21]. Enormous efforts have been undertaken to solve these problems, such as heteroatom doping [22,23] and heterojunction construction [12,24,25]. It is well known that construction of heterojunction is the most effective way for both promoting charge carrier separation and extending the absorption range to visible light, which origins from the building of internal electric fields.

The Z-scheme photocatalytic system is an efficient approach to promote the separation of photogenerated charge [26]. Furthermore, this system could maximize the redox potential of the heterojunction, which is benefit to the degradation of antibiotics [27]. Usually, the Pt, Au and Ag are used as an electron mediator to promote the construction of the Z-scheme system [26,28,29]. However, the noble metal are expensive and rare, which limits large-scale applications in Z-scheme system. Nowadays, nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have already shown immense potential to play a vital role in photocatalysis because their high electron transfer capability and excellent photochemistry properties. Hence, the N-CQDs may act as an electron mediator in the Z-scheme system. Moreover, N-CQDs possess some superiorities than carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based materials coupling with other materials, such as induce charge delocalization and effectively enhance the absorption of light [30,31]. Noteworthily, the molecular oxygen activation of photocatalyst could be promoted by N-CQDs on which surface exists an abundance of surface defect sites [30]. Ag3PO4 is also a rising star due to its super photo-oxidative capabilities for antibiotics under visible light irradiation [32]. However, photocorrosion and rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs are two main drawbacks restrict its application in photocatalysis [33]. The N-CQDs serving as the electron acceptor could inhibit the reduction of silver ions and promote the photocatalytic stability of Ag3PO4, which is an effective method to solve this dilemma [31,34].

In this study, a novel Z-scheme BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 was synthesized via a solvothermal-precipitation method. The structures, morphologies and optical-electronic properties of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials are investigated in detail. The photocatalytic activities of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials are evaluated by photodegradation of antibiotic tetracycline (TC). The mechanism of photocatalytic activities is proposed by applying active species trapping experiments and ESR analysis. It possesses excellent interfacial charge transfer, improved molecular oxygen activation ability and increased light harvesting capacity so as to enhance the photocatalytic activity.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), silver nitrate (≥99.8%), Sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4·2H2O, 97%), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, ≥99%), ammonium citrate, tetracycline were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as received from commercial suppliers without further purification. Solutions were freshly prepared with deionized water (18.25 MΩ cm specific resistance).

N-CQDs was synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method reported in previous work [35]. N-CQDs/BiVO4 were synthesized through a hydrothermal method. Typically, 5 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 10 mL of 2 M HNO3 to form a clear solution. Then, 10 mL of 2 M NaOH containing NH4VO3 (5 mmol) was added into the above solution under vigorous stirring to form a yellow suspension. After that 3 mL of N-CQDs aqueous solution was added and the pH of mixed solution was adjusted to 7 with 2 M NaOH. Next, ultrasound and stirring for 30 min, respectively. The obtained suspension was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained product was cool down to room temperature and washed with ethanol and deionized water several times. The obtained samples were dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight. The pure BiVO4 sample was synthesized under the same conditions without N-CQDs [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4/BiVO4 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials were fabricated as follows: 0.2 g N-CQDs/BiVO4 was dispersed into 50 mL deionized water and the solution was sonicated for 30 min. Subsequently, the different volumes of AgNO3 was added quickly and stirred for 30 min in dark. Then the aqueous solution contained Na2HPO4 (the molar ratio of AgNO3: Na2HPO4 is 3:1) was added into the mixed solution by drop. After stirred for 12 h in dark, the products were collected and washed, respectively. Finally, the obtained samples were dried in a vacuum oven for 12 h. The weight ratios of Ag3PO4 to N-CQDs/BiVO4 were 1, 5, 10 and 20 wt%, and the obtained BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 were labeled as BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-1, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-5, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-20, respectively. For comparison, Ag3PO4/BiVO4 was fabricated by adding the BiVO4 instead of N-CQDs/BiVO4 sample under the same condition.

The crystal structure of the as-prepared samples were characterized by an X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) using Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation in the 2θ ranging from 5 to 90° at a scan rate of 5° min−1 . The chemical composition and chemical states of the as-prepared samples were studied by X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) on ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The as-prepared samples were characterized by a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) using Nicolet 6700 spectrophotometer in the scan range of 400–4000 cm−1. Raman spectra were recorded using the Horiba Jobin Yvon Lab RAM HR800 at room temperature with a 532 nm solid laser as the exciting source. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and energy dispersive spectroscope (EDS) were operated on a FEI-quanta 200F scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were taken on a JEOL JEM-2100F (200 kV) transmission electron microscope. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) using BaSO4 as a reflectance standard. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with an Edinburgh FLsp 920 transient fluorescence spectrometer. The electrolyte was 0.01 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. The photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) experiments were conducted using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660D, Shanghai Chenhua Ltd., China) in a conventional three-electrode configuration. The total organic carbon (TOC) assays were experimented using a Shimadzu TOC-VCPH analyzer. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of radicals spin-trapped were operated on a Bruker model ESR E500 spectrometer by spin-trap reagent DMPO (Sigma Chemical Co.) in methanol and water, respectively.

The photocatalytic activities of BiVO4/N-CQDs-3/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials were evaluated by the photodegradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) under visible light. A 300 W Xe lamp (Perfect Light, wavelength range: 320 nm ≤ λ ≤ 780 nm, light intensity: 160mW/cm2) was used as the light source with a 420 nm cutoff filter to provide visible light irradiation. Experiments were carried out as follows: 30 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL of TC (10 mg/L) in a 250 mL beaker. Before illumination, the suspension was constantly stirred for 30 min in dark until the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was established between TC and photocatalysts. At 5 min intervals, a 5 mL solution was collected and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the photocatalysts. In addition, each experiment was repeated for three times. Then the concentration of TC was detected by recording the absorbance at the characteristic band of 357 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–2550, SHIMADZU, Japan).

The crystallographic features of the as-prepared samples were investigated by XRD, and the results are presented in Fig. 1 . For pure BiVO4, the main peaks can be located at 18.7°, 19.0°, 28.8°, 30.5°,34.5°, 35.2°, 39.8°, 42.5°, 45.6°, 46.7°, 50.3°, 53.3°, 58.5°, 59.3°, which can be indexed to (1 1 0), (0 1 1), ( −1 2 1), (0 4 0), (2 0 0), (0 0 2), (2 1 1), (0 5 1), (2 3 1), (2 4 0), (2 0 2), (1 6 1), (3 2 1), (1 2 3), belonging to the standard monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS NO. 14-0688) [37]. After composited with N-CQDs, the characteristic peaks were similar to that of pure BiVO4, suggesting that the N-CQDs did not significantly change the structure of BiVO4. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of N-CQDs species does not appeared due to the good dispersion, small dosage amount and relatively low diffraction intensity of N-CQDs in the composite [35]. After the deposition of Ag3PO4, the peaks of cubic Ag3PO4 (JCPDS file No. 06-0505) at 2θ of 29.7°, 33.3°, 36.6°, 47.8° and 55.0° were detected from the XRD patterns of Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and Ag3PO4/N-CQDs/BiVO4 [33]. The diffraction peaks of the as-prepared samples were intense, indicating the highly crystalline nature of the as-prepared samples.

The surface chemical states of the pure BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum of the Fig. 2 a indicated the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials mainly contain Bi, V, O, Ag, P, N and C elements while the pure BiVO4 only contains Bi, V and O elements. The high resolution spectra of Bi 4f, V 2p, O 1s, Ag 3d, P 2p, N 1s and C 1s of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials are presented in Fig. 2b–h. As shown in Fig. 2b, the binding energies at 163.37 eV and 158.37 eV could be ascribed to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2. The XPS spectrum of V 2p (Fig. 2c) shows the binding energy at 524.37 eV for V 2p1/2 and 516.79 eV for V 2p3/2, respectively. As for O 1s (Fig. 2d), the main peaks could be decomposed into three peaks. The peaks centered at 532.32 eV, 530.27 eV and 529.72 eV were attributed to CO/CO, the hydroxyl groups (OAH) and BiO bonds of (Bi2O2)2+ in BiVO4, respectively [27,37]. With respect to Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 2e), the peaks of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 at 373.19 eV and 367.2 eV are assigned to Ag+ of Ag3PO4. Fig. 4f shows that the binding energy peak located at 132.74 eV could be assigned to the P5+ in Ag3PO4. As for the N 1s XPS spectrum, the peak at 401.09 eV comes from the nitrogen hydrogen bond in N-CQDs [35]. And the peak of C 1s for BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials can be separated into three peaks. As shown in Fig. 2e, the separated peaks located at 287.86 eV, 286.17 eV and 284.76 eV could be assigned to the CN, CO and graphitic (CC and CC), respectively [30,37–40]. The XPS results further demonstrated that the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials were successfully synthesized.

The Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR) spectra of BiVO4, Ag3PO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials are displayed in Fig. 3 (a). The broad absorption peaks around 3210 cm−1 are attributed to OH groups vibrations of adsorbed H2O [41]. The FTIR spectrum of BiVO4 exhibits a strong characteristic peak at 731 cm−1, which ascribed to υ1 (VO4) and υ3 (VO4) [42]. For Ag3PO4, the band at 553 cm−1 and 1011 cm−1 could be ascribed to the typical stretching vibration of OPO rings and POP groups, respectively [32]. Meanwhile, the bands at about 1670 cm−1 may assign to HOH bending vibrations of adsorbed H2O [37]. For N-CQDs/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4, the band at 1648 cm−1 and 1555 cm−1 could be ascribed to the vibrational absorption of CO and NH, respectively [30,39].

The composition and structure of the photocatalysts could be also confirmed by Raman spectra. The Raman spectra of BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials are presented in Fig. 3b. The Raman spectrum of BiVO4 displays five Raman peaks at 212.8, 327.0, 366.6, 716.9 and 826.3 cm−1, which further proves that BiVO4 is monoclinic phase. The Raman spectra of N-CQDs/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 reveal that two characteristic peaks of N-CQDs at 1386 and 1609 cm−1 are correspond to d-band (the sp3 defects of carbon atoms) and G-band (the sp2-bonded carbon atoms), respectively [39,43]. The Raman spectra of Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 exhibit two different characteristic peaks compared with the Raman spectrum of BiVO4. The peak at 918 cm−1 could be ascribed to the virbration of PO4 3− [44]. The peak at 569 cm−1 could be assigned to the symmetric stretch of POP bonds [32,44]. The Raman spectrum of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials shows a combination of characteristic bands of BiVO4, N-CQDs and Ag3PO4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and textural property of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure and morphology of the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials are characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and HRTEM (Fig. 6 ). The TEM image of BiVO4 in Fig. 6a displays irregular plate-like morphology with average diameter about 2–3 μm. The HRTEM image of BiVO4 (Fig. 6d) shows the lattice space of 0.24 nm is corresponding to (0 2 0) crystallographic planes of the monoclinic BiVO4 [37]. The TEM image of Ag3PO4 in Fig. 6b indicates the obtained Ag3PO4 is sphere-like shape and these nanoparticles reunion together with each other. As shown in the HRTEM image of Ag3PO4 (Fig. 6e), the lattice space of Ag3PO4 can be identified as 0.27 nm indexed to the (2 1 0) plane of cubic Ag3PO4 [7]. The TEM image of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 is shown in Fig. 6c, indicating that the BiVO4 and Ag3PO4 were combined with each other. Moreover, the HRTEM image of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 is also presented (Fig. 6f). The measured lattice spaces of 0.24, 0.33, 0.27 nm match well with the (0 2 0) plane of monoclinic BiVO4, (0 0 2) plane of N-CQDs and (2 1 0) plane of Ag3PO4, respectively [7,35,37]. The intimate contact of BiVO4, N-CQDs and Ag3PO4 is beneficial to charge transport and photocatalytic activity.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were analyzed to study the optical properties of the photocatalysts. The UV–vis DRS of the pure BiVO4, Ag3PO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials are presented in Fig. 7 a. The pure BiVO4 and Ag3PO4 have the optical absorption edge at approximately about 550 nm and 510 nm, respectively, which agrees with the previous reports [27,46]. Compared with pure BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4 hybrid materials exhibits stronger visible light absorptions due to the Ag3PO4 nanoparticles loaded on the surface of BiVO4 and Ag3PO4 holds high visible absorption performance [46]. Noteworthily, the absorption intensity of N-CQDs/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials in the whole region apparently enhanced due to the N-CQDs could act as a photosensitizer and work as photocenter for absorbing light [35]. Obviously, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 possessed the best absorption performance in the range of visible light. The results indicate that BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials could greatly enhance optical absorption in visible light due to synergistic effect among BiVO4, N-CQDs and Ag3PO4, which is beneficial to enhance the photocatalytic activity.

Generally, the band gap energy (E g) of BiVO4 and Ag3PO4 are calculated using equation αhυ = Φ (hυ – E g) N/2, in which α, h, υ, Φ and E g are absorption coefficient, planck constant (Φ = 1), light frequency, proportionality constant and band gap energy, respectively [47]. The value of n is decided by the characteristics of the semiconductor. As the BiVO4 is a direct band semiconductor and the value of n is 1, while the Ag3PO4 is an indirect band semiconductor and the value of n is 4 [11,48]. Thus, the E g value of BiVO4 and Ag3PO4 are about 2.38 eV and 2.20 eV, respectively, which are calculated by extrapolation of the linear parts of the curves (Fig. 7b and c). These results are in accordance with previous reports [29,47]. Furthermore, the band edge positions of the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of BiVO4 and Ag3PO4 can be calculated by the empirical equations: E VB = χ – E e + 0.5 E g and E CB = E VB - E g, in which χ is the electronegativity of the semiconductor, which is the geometric average of the absolute electronegativity of the constituent atoms (χ values of BiVO4 and Ag3PO4 are 6.16 eV and 5.96 eV, respectively) [11,48]; E e is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV) [48]; and E g is the band gap energy of the semiconductor. Consequently, the E VB and the E CB of BiVO4 are 2.85 eV and +0.47 eV, respectively. And those of Ag3PO4 are 2.56 eV and +0.36 eV, respectively.

To explore the separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, the photoluminescence (PL) was performed [27]. In general, the low intensity of PL indicates that the good separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs [35]. The PL spectra of BiVO4, Ag3PO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials are shown in Fig. 7d. Upon the excitation wavelength at 450 nm, all samples have strong emissions peaked at around 502 nm. The intensity of the PL spectra of BiVO4 is the greatest, which was decreased in the presence of Ag3PO4 and N-CQDs. Obviously, the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials showed significantly dropped PL intensity when compared with the pure and binary photocatalysts, which suggests that the cooperative effects between Ag3PO4 and N-CQDs on the surface of BiVO4 can improve the separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

To further study the process of electron tansfer, the transient photocurrent response curves of pure BiVO4, Ag3PO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials were measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 a. All photoelectrodes exhibit a quick response under visible light irradiation, while the photocurrent shows a dramatic decrease when the light turned off. Obviously, the transient photocurrent intensity of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials presents a remarkable improvement of photoresponse performance compared to Ag3PO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4 and Ag3PO4/BiVO4, inferring that the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials hold a highest separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

The EIS analysis was used to evaluate the efficiency of separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [49]. Generally, a smaller semicircle in the EIS Nyquist plot indicated a smaller charge-transfer resistance. The EIS Nyquist plot of pure BiVO4, Ag3PO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials depicted in Fig. 8b. Obviously, the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials showed smallest semicircle at high frequencies among all the as-prepared photocatalysts, implying that the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials possess highest separation and electron transfer efficiency of electron-hole pairs.

To evaluate the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials under visible light irradiation, the tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) was chosen as a representative refractory pollutant. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the as-prepared samples could photodegrade tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) gradually as the irradiation time increased and the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10 hybrid materials exhibited much better photocatalytic performance than the other samples for TC degradation. After irradiation for 30 min, 88.9% of TC can be photodegraded by BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10 while only 28.9% of TC can be photodegraded by pure BiVO4, implying that introduction of N-CQDs and Ag3PO4 on the surface of BiVO4 was an efficient strategy to promote photocatalytic performance. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10 induced by enhanced visible light absorption and improved separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs which was confirmed by UV–vis spectrum and PL emission spectra, respectively. However, the degradation efficiency of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-20 is lower than that of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10. The possible reasons can be ascribed to the excessive amounts of the Ag3PO4 accumulated together may shade the surface of N-CQDs/BiVO4, which leads to the poor optical properties of light absorption and less active sites. The results above indicated the formation of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials and the synergistic effects between each component, which drastically enhanced the photocatalytic performance.

The reaction kinetics for photodegradation of TC are studied by the pseudo-first-order kinetic model [50]:
(1)
- ln
C /
C 0
=
k 1
t
where C0 and C are the initial TC concentration and instant concentration, and k1 is the pseudo-first-order kinetic reaction kinetic values (k) (min−1). Additionally, the reaction kinetics are studied by the pseudo-second-order kinetic model [51]:
(2)
1 / C - 1 /
C 0
=
k 2
t
where C0 and C are the initial TC concentration and instant concentration, and k2 is the pseudo-second-order kinetic reaction kinetic values (k) (min−1). According to the obtained data, photocatalytic degradation of TC was best fitted with pseudo-first-order kinetics and the results are shown in Fig. 9b. The apparent rate constants for BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-1, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-5, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-20 are 0.01186, 0.02552, 0.03953, 0.04686, 0.05398, 0.07097 and 0.05975 min−1, respectively. The k value for TC degradation over BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10 hybrid materials is about 6.00, 2.78 and 1.80 times higher than that of BiVO4, N-CQDs/BiVO4, Ag3PO4/BiVO4, respectively. These results demonstrated that the formation of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials could greatly promote the photocatalytic activity of BiVO4.

The mineralization ability is also an essential parameter to evaluate the photocatalytic performance. Table 1 presented the TOC removal by BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10. After 90 min irradiation, 24.3% and 59.8% of TOC was removed by BiVO4 and BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10, respectively. Thus, BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4-10 exhibited a very desirable mineralization ability to TC and promising application potential for wastewater treatment.

To study the recyclability of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials, the photocatalytic degradation processes were repeated for five times under the same conditions and the results are shown in Fig. 10 a. After five recycles, the removal efficiency of TC based on BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials still remained at 79.9%, revealing the high stability and good performance of the as-prepared sample. To further investigate the chemical stability of the sample after five recycles, the XRD was conducted. As shown in Fig. 10b, the crystalline phase of the used BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials nearly stays the same. All of these results above demonstrated that the photocatalysts remains stable.

To deeply understand the intrinsic mechanism of the photocatalytic process, the active species trapping experiments were conducted. In this study, three different quenchers, 1, 4-benzoquinone (BQ,
O2 − scavengers), triethanolamine (TEA, h+ scavengers) and isopropanol (IPA,
OH scavengers) were adopted [14,52–54]. The concentrations of these scavengers in the photocatalytic system were 1 mmol/L. As presented in Fig. 11 , the photodegradation efficiency of TC decreased seriously when the addition of BQ, meaning that
O2 − plays crucial role in the photocatalytic process. Similarly, IPA and TEA also shows effect on the photocatalytic activity, which indicates that both
OH and h+ took part in the photocatalytic oxidation process. According to above results, it is ascertained that all the active species (
O2 −,
OH and h+) have an influence on the photocatalytic reaction and the order of the influence is
O2 − > h+ >
OH.

To further evaluate and confirm the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation by the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials, the ESR experiments with 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were conducted under visible light irradiation (Fig. 12 ). The signal intensity of DMPO-
O2 − are presented in Fig. 12a, no ESR signals were detected under the dark condition. However, six characteristic peaks of the DMPO-
O2− species appeared under visible light irradiation for 5 and 10 min, demonstrating that BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials could facilitate produce
O2− under the visible light irradiation. As for DMPO-
OH, four characteristic peaks were showed up under visible light irradiation and no signals appeared under the dark (Fig. 12b). However, the signals of
OH are weaker than that of
O2−. This phenomenon validates that both the
O2− and
OH were generated by BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 in the photodegradation process. Moreover, the
O2− radicals has a greater impact on photodegradation process than the
OH radicals. Consequently, according to the results of the active species trapping experiments and this ESR analysis, it can be confirmed that the
O2− and
OH played an extremely important role in the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 photodegradation process.

On the basis of all the results and analyses, a possible charge separation mechanism for photocatalytic degradation over BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials is proposed and presented in Scheme 1 . Conventionally, the type-II Ag3PO4/BiVO4 heterojunction construct a double-transfer mechanism. Under visible light irradiation, the photogenerated electrons of Ag3PO4 will migrate to BiVO4 and the photogenerated holes will transfer to Ag3PO4 [55]. However, this traditional model goes against with formation of active species. These accumulated electrons in the CB of BiVO4 cannot reduce O2 to generate
O2− due to the CB edge potential is more positive than the reduction potential of O2/
O2− (−0.33 eV vs NHE). Hence, the enhanced photoactivity of the type-II heterojunction photocatalyst mainly owing to high electron-hole pairs separation efficiency. However, the redox ability of the photocatalyst is sacrificed because the reduction and oxidation processes occur on the semiconductor with the lower reduction and oxidation potentials, respectively. From the above analysis, we explained the enhanced photocatalytic activity of BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 may ascribe to the formation of Z-scheme system, increased light harvesting capacity and improved molecular oxygen activation ability. As the N-CQDs holds excellent electrons collect and transfer ability [31,56,57], the electrons on the CB of BiVO4 quickly migrated to N-CQDs when N-CQDs was introduced to form BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 Z-scheme system. Therefore, the N-CQDs could act as charge separation center and result in an enhanced photocatalytic activity. Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons on the N-CQDs could be rapidly combined with the holes on the VB of Ag3PO4. Thus,
OH was produced from H2O by the photogenerated holes on the VB of BiVO4, which is essential to photodegrade the organics. Meanwhile, the accumulated electrons on CB of Ag3PO4 also could be captured by N-CQDs. The electrons on the surface of N-CQDs could reduce O2 to yield
O2− due to an abundance of surface defect sites on the N-CQDs could be effectively refreshed by the visible light irradiation [30,40]. In addition, the N-CQDs could act as a photosensitizer and work as an antenna to enhance visible light absorption. In a word, all of these photo-induced reactive species and the high visible light absorption are together responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic activity.

A novel nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) mediated Ag3PO4/BiVO4 Z-scheme photocatalyst was synthesized for the first time. The N-CQDs and Ag3PO4 were anchored on the surface of BiVO4 by chemical bonds. The optimum BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation, showing 88.9% removal efficiency of TC (10 mg/L) in 30 min and 59.8% mineralization in 90 min. The excellent photocatalytic degradation performance based on the BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials can be explained by the following reasons. (1) The Z-scheme system was established ascribed to the N-CQDs holds excellent electron transfer and collect ability. Compared with the type-II Ag3PO4/BiVO4 heterojunction, the Z-scheme system holds some superiorities [28,55]. It not only brings about the improved separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs but also maintains the strong redox ability of these photogenerated charges; (2) the introduction of N-CQDs and Ag3PO4 on the surface of BiVO4 can result in improved visible-light absorption ability. Especially, the N-CQDs could act as a photosensitizer and work as photocenter for absorbing visible light; (3) the molecular oxygen activation ability of N-CQDs may make a contribution to the excellent photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The high degradation efficiencies towards TC by BiVO4/N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid materials and a profound understanding of its mechanism, demonstating the great potential for practical applications in further wastewater treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51521006, No. 51739004 and No. 21776066).
